skill.name: self-improving-agent
skill.description: Helps an AI agent record lessons, extract reusable patterns, and update skill guidance across Claude, Codex, and Claude Code workflows.
skill.summary: Records agent experiences as reusable learning patterns and guided skill improvements.
skill.author: charon-fan

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced many alerts, but most blocker-level items are false positives from markdown examples, diagrams, and research links. The confirmed risk is high because this community skill requests broad file-editing and Bash capabilities, teaches self-modification of skills, and documents hooks that can log tool input and command output.

high_findings.title: Broad Self-Modifying Agent Permissions
high_findings.description: The community skill requests Bash, Write, Edit, and WebSearch access while instructing the agent to update skill files, memory files, and related guidance after skill events. This is powerful automation, but it can change repository behavior beyond the immediate user task.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The requested tools and self-update workflow are explicit in the skill metadata and process description. The intent appears productivity-focused, but the modification scope is broad.

high_findings.title: Hook Configuration Can Log Sensitive Tool Data
high_findings.description: The hook examples pass tool input and Bash output into local shell commands for logging. Tool input and output can include secrets, paths, or proprietary data, so this pattern needs explicit user approval and log handling controls.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The hook command arguments are shown directly in the documentation. I did not find evidence of network exfiltration, but sensitive local logging remains a real risk.

medium_findings.title: Home Directory Configuration Changes
medium_findings.description: The documentation instructs users to create a symlink under ~/.claude and edit Claude Code settings. This is a legitimate installation pattern, but it changes global agent behavior for the user account.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The filesystem paths and symlink command are explicit. The risk is contextual because users may expect installation steps to touch Claude configuration.

low_findings.title: Shell Command Findings Are Mostly Documentation
low_findings.description: Most external command detections are markdown examples, diagrams, or hook configuration snippets. I did not find unquoted command construction or instructions to execute untrusted input through a shell.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited command patterns are visible in markdown and JSON examples. The remaining concern is operational exposure when users wire those hooks into their environment.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Research Links, Not Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs point to research references and articles. I did not find evidence that the skill sends local data to those URLs.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are presented as citations and reference links. No code path or instruction was found that posts user data to them.

low_findings.title: Weak Crypto, C2, and Credential Alerts Appear False Positive
low_findings.description: The weak cryptography, C2 keyword, Windows SAM, and reconnaissance alerts match prose, diagrams, markdown paths, or article titles. No evidence found of credential theft, cryptographic downgrade, C2 behavior, or system reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found documentation and example content at these locations, not executable malicious behavior. The alerts are explainable by keywords in benign text.

dangerous_patterns.title: Broad Self-Modifying Agent Permissions
dangerous_patterns.description: The community skill requests Bash, Write, Edit, and WebSearch access while instructing the agent to update skill files, memory files, and related guidance after skill events. This is powerful automation, but it can change repository behavior beyond the immediate user task.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The requested tools and self-update workflow are explicit in the skill metadata and process description. The intent appears productivity-focused, but the modification scope is broad.

dangerous_patterns.title: Hook Configuration Can Log Sensitive Tool Data
dangerous_patterns.description: The hook examples pass tool input and Bash output into local shell commands for logging. Tool input and output can include secrets, paths, or proprietary data, so this pattern needs explicit user approval and log handling controls.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The hook command arguments are shown directly in the documentation. I did not find evidence of network exfiltration, but sensitive local logging remains a real risk.

content.user_title: Improve AI Skills With Learning Memory
content.value_statement: AI agents often repeat the same mistakes across tasks. This skill records lessons, extracts patterns, and guides future skill updates.
actual_capabilities: Defines semantic, episodic, and working memory structures for agent learning.
Guides extraction of reusable patterns from completed skill sessions.
Provides workflows for correcting inaccurate skill guidance after errors.
Documents evolution markers that trace skill changes to source episodes.
Includes optional Claude Code hook examples for session and Bash event logging.
limitations: Requires broad file-editing permissions to update skills and memory files.
Hook examples can expose sensitive tool input or command output in logs.
Does not include an independent validation engine for learned patterns.
Research links and examples may become outdated over time.

use_cases.title: Improve Team Agent Playbooks
use_cases.description: Convert repeated task lessons into reusable skill guidance with source markers.

use_cases.title: Track Debugging Lessons
use_cases.description: Record recurring fixes and promote reliable patterns into debugger guidance.

use_cases.title: Review Skill Quality Over Time
use_cases.description: Use validation templates and confidence scores to decide which patterns remain useful.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize One Session
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review after a completed task.
prompt_templates.prompt: Review the latest skill session. List what happened, what worked, what failed, and one reusable lesson.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Reusable Patterns
prompt_templates.scenario: Intermediate pattern creation from several related sessions.
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these recent sessions. Extract patterns that apply across tasks, then assign confidence and target skills.

prompt_templates.title: Correct Skill Guidance
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced correction after guidance caused an error.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find the guidance that contributed to this failure. Propose a correction marker, validation steps, and affected skills.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Learning Memory
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced maintenance of long-running agent memory.
prompt_templates.prompt: Audit semantic and episodic memory. Identify stale patterns, conflicting guidance, missing evidence, and updates needing human approval.

output_examples.input: A debugging session found that callback chains caused stale refresh behavior.
output_examples.output: The skill would summarize the episode, name the reusable pattern, set confidence, and recommend updates for debugger guidance.

output_examples.input: A user reports that a new pattern caused confusing PRD requirements.
output_examples.output: The skill would lower confidence, create a correction note, and recommend keeping the pattern limited until validated.

output_examples.input: A team wants to review all learned patterns from the week.

best_practices: Review every proposed skill change before applying it to shared repositories.
Keep confidence scores tied to clear evidence and user feedback.
Disable hook logging when tool input or output may contain secrets.
anti_patterns: Do not auto-apply broad skill changes from a single weak example.
Do not log command output from sensitive environments.
Do not treat research citations as proof that a pattern is valid.

faq.question: Does this skill execute code by itself?
faq.answer: The markdown describes workflows and hook configuration. Execution depends on user wiring and granted tool permissions.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: It asks for broad editing and Bash access, and it can guide changes to other skills and memory files.

faq.question: Can it work with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code?
faq.answer: The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code. Hook examples are specific to Claude Code.

faq.question: Does it send data to external services?
faq.answer: No evidence found of data exfiltration. The network findings are research links and WebSearch capability.

faq.question: What should users review before installation?
faq.answer: Users should review tool permissions, hook logging, symlink setup, and every proposed skill update.

faq.question: Is human approval still needed?
faq.answer: Yes. Human review is important because learned patterns can be wrong, stale, or too broad.
